dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a priority one choking at 1234 Main St.
Hi, I'm Daniel, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing for the last week, and today I felt like a pill might be stuck in my throat.
Are you experiencing any other symptoms besides difficulty breathing or a sensation of a pill being stuck?
Yes, my breathing is really fast and I have a productive cough. I also feel dizzy and lightheaded.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your name?
What is the name of the county we're in?
And what brought you to call 911 today?
How old are you?
Open your eyes if you're comfortable, Mr. Smith.
Mr. Smith, let me check your throat briefly. Your airway looks clear and patent.
Mr. Smith, please breathe normally while I assess your lungs.
I want you to take a deep breath so I can listen better.
Your breathing is rapid but your airway remains unobstructed.
Respirations are at 26 per minute and shallow.
Pulse is strong at 88 beats per minute and regular.
Blood pressure is 121 over 85.
Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
Pulse is 88 and regular.
Your skin appears warm, dry, and normal in color.
Pupils are equal and reactive.
Upon auscultation, there is bilateral expiratory wheezing in the lower lobes.
Have you been using any medications for your breathing lately?
I took a medication pill this morning and felt like it might be stuck in my throat.
How long has this difficulty been going on?
About a week. I've been managing with OTC medications at home but they haven't helped.
Do you have any history of chronic conditions related to the heart or lungs?
I have pulmonary hypertension and congestive heart failure. Also, hyperlipidemia, gout, and osteoarthritis.
You are fully alert and oriented with a GCS score of 15.
Were you given any medical treatment for this type of breathing difficulty previously?
I haven't been on any specific prescriptions.
We're beginning a nebulizer to help with your breathing. It contains albuterol and ipratropium.
Performing a 4-lead ECG monitoring now for baseline.
A 12-lead ECG is showing a paced rhythm at 74 BPM with no ST segment changes.
I'll be placing a 20G IV in your left forearm to allow for decreased dosing delays.
Taking your blood glucose now.
Your blood glucose reading is 129.
Albuterol of 5mg and ipratropium of 0.5mg have been administered via nebulizer with oxygen at 6 liters per minute.
Confirming the dose and medication combination is correct.
Nebulizer treatment has finished. Next, we'll be giving you a 10mg IV dose of dexamethasone to help manage airway inflammation.
Rechecking oxygen saturation-it's now 100%, up from previous 99%.
Your breathing has slowed to 20 per minute, and you're no longer in acute distress.
Pulse is now at 83 and blood pressure has decreased to 111/80.
We are taking you to [COMPANY_NAME] under protocol 3-11. You have shown a positive response to treatment-oxygen saturation has risen, and no pain was reported.
Securing you to the stretcher with a 5-point harness and placing you in a seated non-emergency position.
Mr. Smith is being transferred to [COMPANY_NAME] Bed 18. He shows a good response to treatment, no signs of hypoxia, and remains fully oriented.
Earlier, you mentioned lightheadedness but no chest pain-was there any chest discomfort?
No, I have no chest pain or nausea.
And your skin is still warm as previously noted?
Yes, it remains the same; no changes.
You are still responding appropriately and there's no change in mental status.
Your latest blood pressure remains at 111/80.
Skin remains warm and unchanged when rechecked.
Oxygen saturation remains at 100% on room air.
Let me double-check that you're stable with no signs of distress.
Everything is secure and ready; we're prepared for transport.
Transport is non-emergent to [COMPANY_NAME].
Mr. Smith, you will remain in an upright and secure seat during the transfer.
Upon arrival, we will hand off a complete report to the ED nurse on duty.
Just to confirm, he has no listed allergies and no known prescribed meds for this condition.
Recheck skin shows no changes-texture and color remain stable.
No further medications are needed at this point; he has responded well to the initial doses.
Equipment and IV lines are secure and functioning properly.
Ground transport will take us to [COMPANY_NAME] in approximately 15 minutes.
Final round of vitals before arrival.
Breathing remains stable at 20 per minute.
Pulse is at 83 and steady.
No significant issues during transit and vitals remain in normal range.
Arrival will be in about 14 minutes; a full report will be ready for the receiving team.
Patient is on board, aware, and comfortable in the seated position.
ED staff has been notified of the transport and arrival time.
Mr. Smith is a 53-year-old male with a known history of pulmonary hypertension and related heart conditions.
All preparations are complete; he's ready for transfer.
I will confirm all vitals, meds, and documentation during the final check before ED arrival.
Noted that he has no known allergies, and medications are limited to over-the-counter.
No trauma or changes observed during transport.
EKG remains consistently paced at 74-no new abnormalities.
Stable throughout-with no sign of deterioration or distress.
Double-checking reports and IV notes for accuracy and completeness prior to arrival at [COMPANY_NAME].
I will provide a summary of the protocol and medications given for final call documentation.
Transport conditions remain uneventful.
At [COMPANY_NAME], kindly reassess as per protocols upon arrival for confirmation.
Safe arrival confirmed, vitals and EKG on file, and patient stable.
Authorized transfer to Bed 18. Receiving nurse is aware and prepared.
Let the receiving RN know we ensured airway, breathing, and circulation were stable throughout.
Patient is alert and responded well to initial medications.
Transport is complete; the ED team has taken over your care.
His medical history includes over-the-counter administering during this episode and symptoms mentioned.
No pain reported at any time-documented in every reassessment cycle.
All transmitted to [COMPANY_NAME] and included in the EPCR documentation.
Final handoff equipment and documentation are complete and verified on site.
Patient was handed off clearly and is alert and stable.
All allergy and history notes are correctly transferred to the receiving staff.
No status changes occurred during the call or upon arrival. All transport details included in record.
No pain noted in assessments-no additional meds required.
Confirmed correct bed and any noted delays are recorded in the narrative.
Transport is officially closed after handoff and nurse sign off.
Full patient report and documentation have been provided to hospital staff.
Final confirmation from the nurse regarding vitals and meds taken.
Transport has been completed, and the patient remains stable under hospital care.
